quizQuestion: Identify the organ system shown in this illustration.
explanation: This is an overview of the female reproductive system. The ovaries produce ova and sex hormones (estrogen, progesterone). Each ovary connects to the uterus via a fallopian tube; fertilization typically occurs in the ampulla of the fallopian tube. The uterus is the site of implantation and fetal development. The cervix is the narrow lower portion opening into the vagina, the muscular canal to the exterior.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the female reproductive system showing ovaries, fallopian tubes, uterus, cervix, and vagina

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the female reproductive system?

quizQuestion: Identify the organ system shown in this illustration.
explanation: This overview shows the male reproductive system. The testes (in the scrotum) produce sperm and testosterone. Sperm mature in the epididymis, then travel through the vas deferens during ejaculation. The seminal vesicles, prostate gland, and bulbourethral glands add secretions to form semen. The penis delivers semen into the female reproductive tract and also carries urine to the exterior via the urethra.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the male reproductive system showing testes, epididymis, vas deferens, prostate, and penis

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the male reproductive system?

quizQuestion: This diagram correlates hormonal changes with cyclic events in the female reproductive system. What cycle is depicted?
explanation: The menstrual cycle (approximately 28 days) integrates the ovarian cycle and uterine cycle, coordinated by hormones. Rising FSH stimulates follicle development (follicular phase). The LH surge triggers ovulation (~day 14). The ruptured follicle becomes the corpus luteum (luteal phase), secreting progesterone to maintain the endometrium. If no fertilization occurs, the corpus luteum regresses, progesterone falls, and menstruation begins.
visualDescription: Diagram correlating FSH, LH, estrogen, and progesterone levels with follicular development and uterine endometrial changes across a 28-day cycle

variants.quizQuestion: Which image depicts the hormonal and cyclic changes of the menstrual cycle?

quizQuestion: Identify the process of female gamete development illustrated here.
explanation: Oogenesis is the process of producing mature ova. Oogonia (2n) undergo mitosis before birth to form primary oocytes, which arrest in prophase I. At puberty, each menstrual cycle one primary oocyte completes meiosis I to yield a secondary oocyte (n) and a first polar body. Meiosis II completes only upon fertilization, producing the ovum (n) and a second polar body. Thus, one functional egg and up to three polar bodies are produced.
visualDescription: Diagram showing progression from oogonium through primary oocyte, meiosis I arrest, secondary oocyte, and ovum formation with polar bodies

variants.quizQuestion: Which image illustrates oogenesis — the development of female gametes?

quizQuestion: This illustration shows sequential stages within the ovary. What process is depicted?
explanation: Ovarian follicle development (folliculogenesis) proceeds through distinct stages: primordial follicle → primary follicle → secondary follicle → antral (tertiary) follicle → Graafian (preovulatory) follicle. After ovulation, the ruptured follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, which secretes progesterone. If unfertilized, it degenerates into the corpus albicans (fibrous scar tissue).
visualDescription: Cross-section of ovary showing primordial, primary, secondary, and Graafian follicles plus corpus luteum and corpus albicans

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows ovarian follicle development from primordial follicle to corpus luteum?

quizQuestion: Identify the transverse section of the male external genitalia shown here.
explanation: The penis contains three cylinders of erectile tissue: two corpora cavernosa (dorsal pair, larger) and one corpus spongiosum (ventral, surrounds the urethra). During erection, parasympathetic stimulation causes nitric oxide release, relaxing smooth muscle in the helicine arteries, engorging the sinusoids with blood. The tunica albuginea compresses emissary veins, maintaining rigidity. The corpus spongiosum stays softer to keep the urethra patent for ejaculation.
visualDescription: Transverse cross-section of the penis showing corpora cavernosa, corpus spongiosum, urethra, and surrounding fascia layers

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows a cross-section of the penis with corpora cavernosa and corpus spongiosum?

quizQuestion: Identify the structures of the male external genitalia shown in this sectional view.
explanation: The scrotum is a cutaneous sac housing the testes outside the abdominal cavity, keeping them ~2–3°C below core body temperature for optimal spermatogenesis. Key layers from outside in: skin → dartos muscle (wrinkles skin for thermoregulation) → external spermatic fascia → cremaster muscle (elevates testes) → tunica vaginalis (serous sac). The testis itself is covered by the tunica albuginea and divided into lobules containing seminiferous tubules.
visualDescription: Sectional view of the scrotum and testes showing dartos muscle, cremasteric fascia, tunica vaginalis, tunica albuginea, and testicular lobules

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the scrotum and testes with dartos muscle and tunica vaginalis?

quizQuestion: Identify the histological section of the testis showing the sites of sperm production.
explanation: Seminiferous tubules are the site of spermatogenesis. The tubule wall contains Sertoli cells (supporting cells — form the blood-testis barrier, nourish developing sperm, secrete inhibin) and spermatogenic cells at various stages. Between tubules in the interstitium, Leydig cells (interstitial cells) synthesize testosterone in response to LH. Mature sperm are released into the tubule lumen and move to the rete testis, then the epididymis.
visualDescription: Histological cross-sections of seminiferous tubules showing Sertoli cells, spermatogenic cells at various stages, and Leydig cells in the interstitium

variants.quizQuestion: Which histological image shows seminiferous tubules with Sertoli cells and Leydig cells?

quizQuestion: Identify the structure of the male gamete shown in this diagram.
explanation: A spermatozoon has three main parts. The head contains the haploid nucleus (condensed DNA) capped by the acrosome — a lysosome-like vesicle with enzymes (acrosin, hyaluronidase) for penetrating the zona pellucida of the egg. The midpiece is packed with mitochondria arranged in a helix around the axoneme, providing ATP for motility. The tail (flagellum) drives propulsion via the 9+2 axonemal microtubule arrangement powered by dynein ATPase.
visualDescription: Diagram of a sperm cell labeling the acrosome, nucleus (head), mitochondrial sheath (midpiece), and flagellum (tail)

variants.quizQuestion: Which diagram shows the structure of a sperm cell with acrosome, midpiece, and tail?

quizQuestion: Identify the process of male gamete development illustrated here.
explanation: Spermatogenesis occurs in the seminiferous tubules and takes ~74 days. Spermatogonia (2n) undergo mitosis to renew the stem cell pool and produce primary spermatocytes (2n). Each primary spermatocyte undergoes meiosis I to form two secondary spermatocytes (n), then meiosis II to form four spermatids (n). Spermatids then undergo spermiogenesis — a dramatic morphological transformation — to become mature spermatozoa. Unlike oogenesis, all four products are functional.
visualDescription: Diagram showing the stages from spermatogonium through primary and secondary spermatocytes, spermatids, to mature spermatozoa with meiosis I and II indicated

variants.quizQuestion: Which image illustrates spermatogenesis from spermatogonium to spermatozoa?

quizQuestion: Identify the uterine structures and their connections to the ovaries shown here.
explanation: The uterus is a pear-shaped muscular organ with three regions: fundus (dome above fallopian tube openings), body, and cervix. Each fallopian tube has four segments from ovary to uterus: infundibulum (with fimbriae that sweep ova in), ampulla (most common site of fertilization), isthmus, and intramural portion. The broad ligament suspends the uterus and tubes. Ciliated epithelium and smooth muscle peristalsis move the ovum toward the uterus.
visualDescription: Anterior view of the uterus and fallopian tubes with labeled fimbriae, infundibulum, ampulla, isthmus, fundus, body, and cervix

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the uterus with fallopian tubes labeled from fimbriae to the uterine wall?

quizQuestion: Identify the external female genitalia shown in this anatomical illustration.
explanation: The vulva comprises the external female genitalia: mons pubis (fatty pad over pubic symphysis), labia majora (outer folds, homologous to scrotum), labia minora (inner folds, highly vascular), clitoris (erectile tissue, homologous to glans penis), vestibule (space between labia minora containing urethral and vaginal openings), Bartholin's glands (greater vestibular glands — lubricate vestibule, homologous to Cowper's glands), and Skene's glands (periurethral).
visualDescription: Anatomical illustration of the external female genitalia labeling the mons pubis, labia majora, labia minora, clitoris, urethral opening, vaginal opening, and vestibular glands

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the vulva — the external female genitalia?

quizQuestion: This diagram illustrates hormonal control of the male reproductive system. What axis or system is shown?
explanation: The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis regulates male reproduction. GnRH from the hypothalamus stimulates anterior pituitary release of FSH and LH. FSH acts on Sertoli cells to support spermatogenesis and promote inhibin secretion. LH acts on Leydig cells to stimulate testosterone synthesis. Testosterone and inhibin provide negative feedback to the hypothalamus and pituitary, maintaining homeostatic hormone levels. Testosterone drives secondary sex characteristics and anabolism.
visualDescription: Diagram showing the HPG axis with GnRH, FSH and LH pathways, Sertoli and Leydig cell targets, testosterone and inhibin feedback loops

variants.quizQuestion: Which diagram shows the male HPG axis with FSH, LH, testosterone, and inhibin feedback?

quizQuestion: Identify the vascular supply of the kidney shown in this illustration.
explanation: Renal blood flow follows a precise pathway: renal artery → segmental arteries → interlobar arteries (between renal pyramids) → arcuate arteries (arch at corticomedullary junction) → interlobular (cortical radiate) arteries → afferent arterioles → glomerular capillaries → efferent arterioles → peritubular capillaries (cortex) and vasa recta (medulla) → venous system in reverse. The kidneys receive ~20–25% of cardiac output despite being <1% of body mass.
visualDescription: Coronal section of kidney with arterial and venous supply labeled from renal artery through segmental, interlobar, arcuate, and interlobular vessels

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows renal blood flow from renal artery through arcuate and interlobular vessels?

quizQuestion: Identify the internal anatomy of the kidney shown in this coronal section.
explanation: A coronal kidney section reveals: renal cortex (outer, granular — contains glomeruli and convoluted tubules), renal medulla (inner, striated — contains renal pyramids with collecting ducts and loops of Henle), renal columns (cortical tissue between pyramids), renal papilla (apex of each pyramid draining into a minor calyx), minor calyces → major calyces → renal pelvis → ureter. The renal sinus is the central fatty space containing the pelvis, calyces, and vessels.
visualDescription: Coronal section of kidney labeling cortex, medulla, renal pyramids, renal columns, minor and major calyces, renal pelvis, and hilum

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows a kidney cross-section with cortex, medulla, renal pyramids, and calyces?

quizQuestion: Identify the position of the kidneys relative to the abdominal cavity shown here.
explanation: The kidneys are retroperitoneal organs located on the posterior abdominal wall between T12 and L3 vertebrae. The right kidney sits slightly lower than the left (due to the liver above). Each kidney is embedded in perinephric fat within the renal fascia (Gerota's fascia). The adrenal glands sit atop each kidney. Key relations: right kidney contacts liver, duodenum, hepatic flexure of colon; left kidney contacts stomach, pancreas, spleen, splenic flexure.
visualDescription: Anterior view of the abdomen showing bilateral retroperitoneal kidney positions relative to vertebral levels, liver, and other abdominal organs

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the retroperitoneal position of the kidneys relative to the spine and abdominal organs?

quizQuestion: Identify the microcirculation of the kidney functional unit shown here.
explanation: Nephron blood flow: afferent arteriole → glomerulus (high-pressure filtration) → efferent arteriole → peritubular capillaries (cortical nephrons, surrounds proximal/distal tubules for reabsorption/secretion) or vasa recta (juxtamedullary nephrons, thin-walled vessels parallel to loops of Henle enabling countercurrent exchange for urinary concentration). The two-capillary-bed arrangement (glomerulus + peritubular/vasa recta) is unique to the kidney and enables independent regulation of filtration and reabsorption.
visualDescription: Diagram of a nephron with associated blood vessels showing afferent and efferent arterioles, glomerular capillaries, peritubular capillaries, and vasa recta

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows nephron blood flow with afferent/efferent arterioles, peritubular capillaries, and vasa recta?

quizQuestion: Identify the internal cardiac structures that anchor the atrioventricular valves shown here.
explanation: The chordae tendineae ('heartstrings') are fibrous cords connecting the atrioventricular valve leaflets (mitral and tricuspid) to the papillary muscles on the ventricular floor. The papillary muscles contract during systole, tensioning the chordae to prevent valve leaflet prolapse into the atrium (regurgitation). Mitral valve has two papillary muscles (anterolateral and posteromedial); tricuspid has three. Rupture of papillary muscle (e.g., post-MI) causes acute severe mitral regurgitation.
visualDescription: Internal view of the left ventricle showing mitral valve leaflets connected by chordae tendineae to the anterolateral and posteromedial papillary muscles

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows chordae tendineae connecting valve leaflets to papillary muscles?

quizQuestion: Identify the category of structural cardiac abnormalities shown in this illustration.
explanation: Congenital heart defects (CHDs) are structural abnormalities present at birth. Common examples include: ventricular septal defect (VSD — most common CHD), atrial septal defect (ASD), patent ductus arteriosus (PDA — DA fails to close after birth causing L→R shunt), tetralogy of Fallot (VSD + pulmonary stenosis + overriding aorta + RVH — most common cyanotic CHD), and coarctation of the aorta. Left-to-right shunts cause pulmonary hypertension; right-to-left shunts cause cyanosis.
visualDescription: Illustration comparing normal heart structure with common congenital defects such as VSD, ASD, and PDA

variants.quizQuestion: Which image illustrates congenital heart defects including VSD, ASD, and PDA?

quizQuestion: Identify the blood vessels supplying the heart muscle shown in this illustration.
explanation: The coronary arteries arise from the aortic sinuses just above the aortic valve. The left main coronary artery divides into the left anterior descending (LAD — supplies anterior LV, IVS, anterior LV apex) and left circumflex (LCx — supplies lateral and posterior LV). The right coronary artery (RCA) supplies the right heart and, in right-dominant systems (70%), gives the posterior descending artery (PDA). The cardiac veins drain into the coronary sinus → right atrium.
visualDescription: Anterior and posterior views of the heart showing the right coronary artery, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and cardiac veins with the coronary sinus

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the coronary arteries (LAD, LCx, RCA) and cardiac veins on the heart surface?

quizQuestion: Identify the two-loop blood circulation diagram shown here.
explanation: Mammals have a dual (double) circulatory system: pulmonary circulation (right heart → pulmonary arteries → lungs → pulmonary veins → left heart) and systemic circulation (left heart → aorta → body tissues → venae cavae → right heart). These circuits are in series, ensuring all blood is oxygenated before systemic distribution. The right ventricle generates lower pressure (~25/10 mmHg) than the left (~120/80 mmHg), reflecting pulmonary low-resistance vs. systemic high-resistance.
visualDescription: Schematic diagram showing pulmonary and systemic circulation loops with heart, lungs, and body tissues, with arrows indicating oxygenated and deoxygenated blood flow

variants.quizQuestion: Which diagram shows the dual circulatory system with separate pulmonary and systemic loops?

quizQuestion: Identify the internal anatomy of the heart shown in this coronal section.
explanation: A coronal section of the heart reveals four chambers: right atrium (receives deoxygenated blood from SVC/IVC, contains SA node) → tricuspid valve → right ventricle → pulmonary valve → pulmonary trunk; and left atrium (receives oxygenated blood from pulmonary veins) → mitral (bicuspid) valve → left ventricle (thick wall for systemic pressure) → aortic valve → aorta. The interventricular septum separates the ventricles; the interatrial septum separates the atria (contains the fossa ovalis — remnant of foramen ovale).
visualDescription: Coronal section of the heart showing all four chambers, four valves, interatrial and interventricular septa, and major vessels

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the heart's internal anatomy with all four chambers and valves in coronal section?

quizQuestion: Identify the anatomical position of the heart within the chest cavity shown here.
explanation: The heart lies in the middle mediastinum within the pericardial sac, positioned obliquely behind the sternum. It is 2/3 to the left of the midline. Its apex (tip of the left ventricle) points inferiorly and to the left, typically at the 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line — where the apical impulse (point of maximal impulse, PMI) is palpated. The base (great vessel end) faces superiorly and posteriorly. It lies anterior to the esophagus and thoracic aorta, posterior to the sternum.
visualDescription: Anterior thorax illustration showing the heart's position relative to sternum, ribs, lungs, and mediastinal structures with the apex pointing to the left 5th ICS

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the heart's position in the thorax relative to the sternum and lungs?

quizQuestion: Identify the external surface features of the heart shown in this illustration.
explanation: The external heart surfaces include: the sternocostal (anterior) surface dominated by the right ventricle, the diaphragmatic (inferior) surface formed mainly by the left ventricle, and the base (posterior) formed by the atria. The coronary sulcus (atrioventricular groove) encircles the heart separating atria from ventricles and carries the coronary arteries. The anterior and posterior interventricular sulci mark the IVS position. The right and left auricles are ear-like appendages of each atrium.
visualDescription: Anterior and posterior views of the heart exterior showing sulci, auricles, coronary vessels in their grooves, and the great vessels

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the external surface anatomy of the heart with coronary sulcus and interventricular sulci?

quizQuestion: Identify the four cardiac valves shown in this superior view of the heart with the atria removed.
explanation: A superior (basal) view with atria removed reveals all four valves simultaneously. The atrioventricular valves (mitral — bicuspid, left; tricuspid — right) open during diastole to allow ventricular filling. The semilunar valves (aortic — between LV and aorta; pulmonary — between RV and pulmonary trunk) open during systole for ejection. All valves are associated with the fibrous cardiac skeleton (annuli fibrosi), which provides mechanical support, prevents overdistension, and electrically insulates atria from ventricles.
visualDescription: Superior (basal) view of the heart base with atria removed showing the tricuspid, mitral, aortic, and pulmonary valves in their relative positions

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows all four heart valves in a superior view with the atria removed?

quizQuestion: Identify the layers of the heart wall shown in this cross-sectional diagram.
explanation: The heart wall has three layers. The epicardium (visceral pericardium) is the outer serous membrane composed of simple squamous epithelium and connective tissue; the coronary vessels run within it. The myocardium is the thick middle muscular layer of cardiac muscle (uninucleate, striated, branched, connected by intercalated discs with gap junctions for electrical coupling). The endocardium is the thin inner layer of simple squamous epithelium lining the chambers, continuous with the endothelium of vessels. Pericarditis, myocarditis, and endocarditis each affect different layers.
visualDescription: Cross-sectional diagram of the heart wall labeling the epicardium (visceral pericardium), myocardium, endocardium, and the parietal pericardium with pericardial cavity

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the three layers of the heart wall: epicardium, myocardium, and endocardium?

quizQuestion: Identify the type of vascular dilation shown in this medical illustration.
explanation: A fusiform aneurysm is a symmetrical, spindle-shaped dilation involving the entire circumference of the vessel wall, contrasting with a saccular aneurysm (asymmetric outpouching). Most aortic aneurysms are fusiform (abdominal aortic aneurysm, AAA, is most common — often at the infrarenal aorta). Caused by atherosclerosis, hypertension, or connective tissue disorders (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos). Risk of rupture increases with diameter >5.5 cm. Repair options: open surgical or endovascular (EVAR).
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a blood vessel showing symmetric fusiform (spindle-shaped) dilation of the aorta

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows a fusiform aneurysm — a symmetric spindle-shaped vascular dilation?

quizQuestion: Identify the arterial supply to the head and neck shown in this illustration.
explanation: The head and neck receive blood from two main sources: the common carotid arteries (right from the brachiocephalic trunk; left directly from the aortic arch) dividing into external carotid (face/scalp/neck) and internal carotid (brain/orbit); and the vertebral arteries (from subclavian arteries, ascending through transverse foramina C1–C6, merging intracranially to form the basilar artery). The circle of Willis connects internal carotid and vertebrobasilar systems, providing collateral flow.
visualDescription: Lateral and anterior view of the head and neck showing common carotid bifurcation, external and internal carotid arteries, vertebral arteries, and their branches

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the head and neck arteries including carotid and vertebral vessels?

quizQuestion: Identify the organ shown in this detailed medical illustration.
explanation: This detailed illustration of the heart highlights its external and surface anatomy with the coronary vessels. The heart is the muscular pump of the cardiovascular system, beating approximately 100,000 times per day. It weighs 250–350 g in adults. The right side handles deoxygenated blood (pulmonary circuit); the left side handles oxygenated blood (systemic circuit). The cardiac output at rest is ~5 L/min, increasing to 20–25 L/min during intense exercise via increases in heart rate and stroke volume.
visualDescription: High-detail medical illustration of the heart showing external surface with coronary vessels, great vessels, and major anatomical landmarks

variants.quizQuestion: Which detailed illustration shows the heart with coronary vessels and great vessels?

quizQuestion: Identify the plane of section used in this cardiac illustration.
explanation: A sagittal (parasagittal) section of the heart provides a lateral view revealing the relationship between anterior structures (right ventricle, right atrium) and posterior structures (left ventricle, aorta, pulmonary veins). This view is useful for understanding the anterior position of the right ventricle (predisposing it to trauma injury), the posterior position of the left atrium (explaining why left atrial enlargement causes dysphagia), and the spatial relationship between the aorta and pulmonary trunk.
visualDescription: Sagittal or parasagittal section through the heart showing anterior-to-posterior relationships of the cardiac chambers and great vessels

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows a sagittal section through the heart?

quizQuestion: Identify the CNS glial cell shown wrapping myelin around multiple axons.
explanation: Oligodendrocytes are the myelin-producing glial cells of the CNS (contrast: Schwann cells in the PNS). A single oligodendrocyte can myelinate up to 50 axon segments simultaneously, sending out flat processes that wrap around axons. Myelin increases conduction velocity via saltatory conduction (action potentials 'jump' between nodes of Ranvier). Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune demyelinating disease targeting CNS myelin; remyelination by oligodendrocyte precursors is limited.
visualDescription: Illustration of an oligodendrocyte with multiple processes wrapping myelin sheaths around several CNS axons simultaneously

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows an oligodendrocyte myelinating multiple CNS axons?

quizQuestion: Identify the spinal cord level shown in this cross-section based on its characteristic gray and white matter proportions.
explanation: At C5 (cervical enlargement), the spinal cord cross-section shows a large amount of white matter relative to gray matter, reflecting the many ascending and descending tracts passing through this high level. The gray matter has prominent anterior horns (containing large alpha motor neurons for the brachial plexus/upper limb innervation) and smaller posterior horns. The cervical enlargement (C5–T1) supplies the upper limb via the brachial plexus. C5 injury causes deltoid and biceps weakness (shoulder abduction, elbow flexion).
visualDescription: Cross-section of the spinal cord at the C5 cervical level showing large white matter volume, prominent anterior horns, and oval overall shape

variants.quizQuestion: Which cross-section shows the spinal cord at the C5 cervical level with large white matter and prominent anterior horns?

quizQuestion: Identify the spinal cord level shown in this cross-section based on its gray-to-white matter ratio.
explanation: At L1, the spinal cord cross-section shows a relatively rounder shape with a higher gray-to-white matter ratio than cervical levels — fewer ascending/descending tracts remain at this caudal level. The anterior horns are enlarged (lumbar enlargement L1–S3 supplies lower limb via lumbosacral plexus), but smaller than at cervical enlargement. L1 is clinically important: the spinal cord typically ends at L1–L2 (conus medullaris); below this is the cauda equina. L1 compression affects hip flexors and knee extensors.
visualDescription: Cross-section of the spinal cord at the L1 lumbar level showing rounder shape, proportionally larger gray matter relative to white matter compared to cervical levels

variants.quizQuestion: Which cross-section shows the spinal cord at the L1 lumbar level near the conus medullaris?

quizQuestion: Identify the spinal cord level shown in this cross-section with its distinctive thoracic morphology.
explanation: At T2, the spinal cord cross-section has a characteristic rounded oval shape with relatively small gray matter horns and proportionally large white matter. The thoracic cord (T1–T12) has small anterior and posterior horns (few limb motor/sensory neurons) but a unique lateral horn containing preganglionic sympathetic neurons (intermediolateral cell column, IML) — absent in cervical and lumbar cord. This is the hallmark of thoracic cord sections. The thoracic cord is the narrowest part of the cord.
visualDescription: Cross-section of the spinal cord at T2 thoracic level showing oval shape, small anterior and posterior horns, lateral horn (IML), and large white matter columns

variants.quizQuestion: Which cross-section shows the thoracic spinal cord at T2 level with the lateral horn (sympathetic IML)?

acceptableAlternatives: Spinal cord gray and white matter
quizQuestion: Identify the CNS structure shown in this transverse section with butterfly-shaped gray matter.
explanation: The spinal cord in cross-section shows a characteristic H-shaped or butterfly-shaped gray matter (containing neuron cell bodies) surrounded by white matter (myelinated axon tracts). Gray matter horns: anterior (ventral) horn — lower motor neurons; posterior (dorsal) horn — sensory relay neurons; lateral horn (T1–L2, S2–S4) — autonomic preganglionic neurons. White matter columns: dorsal (posterior) columns (fine touch, proprioception), lateral columns (corticospinal, spinothalamic), anterior (ventral) columns (motor).
visualDescription: Transverse cross-section of the spinal cord showing butterfly-shaped gray matter with anterior and posterior horns surrounded by white matter columns

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows a spinal cord cross-section with butterfly-shaped gray matter and surrounding white matter columns?

quizQuestion: Identify the diffuse lymphoid tissue found in mucosal surfaces shown here.
explanation: Mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) is non-encapsulated lymphoid tissue distributed throughout mucosal surfaces. It constitutes the largest component of the immune system by mass. Subtypes include GALT (gut-associated — Peyer's patches in ileum, appendix), BALT (bronchus-associated), NALT (nasal), and MALT of the urogenital tract. MALT contains B cells (especially IgA-secreting plasma cells), T cells, dendritic cells, and macrophages. IgA secretion is the primary mucosal defense mechanism.
visualDescription: Histological illustration of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue in a mucosal surface showing lymphoid follicles, B and T cell zones, and overlying epithelium

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows MALT — mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue with follicles at a mucosal surface?

quizQuestion: Identify the diagram showing the lineage of blood cell development from a common precursor.
explanation: Hematopoiesis occurs primarily in red bone marrow (adults: axial skeleton, proximal long bones). A pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) divides into a common myeloid progenitor (→ erythrocytes, platelets via megakaryocytes, granulocytes, monocytes) or a common lymphoid progenitor (→ B lymphocytes, T lymphocytes, NK cells). Erythropoiesis is stimulated by erythropoietin (EPO) from the kidney. Thrombopoietin drives megakaryocyte/platelet production. G-CSF drives neutrophil production.
visualDescription: Branching diagram showing hematopoietic stem cells differentiating into myeloid and lymphoid lineages with all mature blood cell types at the branch ends

variants.quizQuestion: Which diagram shows hematopoiesis — blood cell development from a pluripotent stem cell?

quizQuestion: Identify the histological section of this secondary lymphoid organ showing follicles and sinuses.
explanation: Lymph node histology shows: outer cortex with lymphoid follicles (primary = quiescent B cells; secondary with germinal centers = active B cell proliferation/somatic hypermutation for affinity maturation), paracortex (deep cortex — T cell zone, also where dendritic cells present antigen), and medulla (medullary cords with plasma cells + macrophages; medullary sinuses filtering lymph). Afferent lymphatics enter the convex surface; efferent lymphatics and vessels exit at the hilum. Sentinel nodes are the first to receive tumor metastases.
visualDescription: Histological section of a lymph node showing the capsule, subcapsular sinus, cortical follicles with germinal centers, paracortex, medullary cords, and hilum

variants.quizQuestion: Which histological image shows a lymph node with follicles, germinal centers, paracortex, and medullary sinuses?

quizQuestion: Identify the blind-ended vessels of the lymphatic system shown here alongside blood capillaries.
explanation: Lymphatic capillaries are blind-ended, thin-walled vessels in the interstitium that absorb excess tissue fluid (lymph), proteins, and cellular debris. They are more permeable than blood capillaries due to overlapping endothelial cells with anchoring filaments that open inter-endothelial junctions when interstitial pressure rises ('one-way valves'). Lacteals are specialized lymphatic capillaries in intestinal villi that absorb dietary fats (chylomicrons) and form chyle. They drain into collecting lymphatics → lymph nodes → trunks → right lymphatic duct or thoracic duct → venous system.
visualDescription: Diagram showing lymphatic capillaries alongside blood capillaries and arterioles in the interstitium, with arrows indicating fluid absorption from tissues

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows lymphatic capillaries as blind-ended vessels absorbing interstitial fluid alongside blood capillaries?

quizQuestion: Identify the body-wide system of vessels and organs shown in this overview illustration.
explanation: The lymphatic system serves three functions: fluid homeostasis (returns ~3 L/day of interstitial fluid to the venous system), fat absorption (lacteals absorb dietary fat in the gut), and immune defense. Major organs: lymph nodes (filter lymph), spleen (filters blood), thymus (T cell maturation), tonsils and MALT (mucosal defense). Lymph flows one-way via intrinsic smooth muscle contractions, extrinsic skeletal muscle compression, and respiratory pressure changes. The thoracic duct (largest lymphatic vessel) drains into the left subclavian vein.
visualDescription: Full-body anterior view showing the lymphatic system including the thoracic duct, major lymph node chains (cervical, axillary, inguinal), spleen, and thymus

variants.quizQuestion: Which full-body illustration shows the lymphatic system with the thoracic duct, lymph nodes, spleen, and thymus?

acceptableAlternatives: Thoracic duct and right lymphatic duct
quizQuestion: Identify the large lymphatic collecting vessels draining into the venous system shown here.
explanation: Lymphatic vessels merge into trunks (lumbar, intestinal, bronchomediastinal, subclavian, jugular), which converge into two terminal ducts. The thoracic duct (the largest) begins at the cisterna chyli (L1–L2), ascends through the thorax, and empties into the left subclavian/internal jugular junction — draining the entire body below the diaphragm, and the left arm and left side of the head/thorax. The right lymphatic duct drains only the right arm and right head/thorax into the right subclavian/jugular junction.
visualDescription: Diagram of the upper body showing lymphatic trunks converging into the thoracic duct (left side) and right lymphatic duct, with drainage territories indicated

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the thoracic duct and right lymphatic duct draining into the venous system at the subclavian junctions?

quizQuestion: Identify the secondary lymphoid organ shown here that filters blood and recycles erythrocytes.
explanation: The spleen is the largest secondary lymphoid organ, weighing ~150 g, located in the left upper quadrant beneath the 9th–11th ribs. It has two functional compartments: white pulp (lymphoid tissue with periarteriolar lymphoid sheaths — T cells, and lymphoid follicles — B cells; mounts immune responses to blood-borne antigens) and red pulp (venous sinusoids and splenic cords — filters blood, removes aged/defective RBCs via erythrophagocytosis by macrophages, stores platelets). Splenectomy increases susceptibility to encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcus, meningococcus, H. influenzae).
visualDescription: External view and internal histological diagram of the spleen showing white pulp (periarteriolar sheaths and follicles) and red pulp (sinusoids and cords)

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the spleen with white pulp and red pulp compartments?

quizQuestion: Identify the primary lymphoid organ shown here that is responsible for T cell maturation.
explanation: The thymus is a bilobed primary lymphoid organ in the superior mediastinum (anterior to the great vessels). Each lobe is subdivided into lobules with two zones: cortex (dense, dark — immature T cells (thymocytes) undergo positive selection for MHC recognition) and medulla (pale — mature T cells undergo negative selection to eliminate self-reactive clones; contains Hassall's corpuscles). The thymus is largest at puberty and involutes with age. Thymic aplasia (DiGeorge syndrome) causes T cell deficiency.
visualDescription: Diagram of the thymus showing bilobed structure, cortex with dense thymocytes, medulla with Hassall's corpuscles, and lobule organization

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the thymus with its cortex and medulla containing Hassall's corpuscles?

quizQuestion: Identify the lymphoid tissue forming the immune ring at the entrance to the pharynx.
explanation: Waldeyer's ring is a ring of lymphoid tissue guarding the entrance to the respiratory and digestive tracts: palatine tonsils (lateral oropharynx — most commonly removed in tonsillectomy), pharyngeal tonsil (adenoid — posterior nasopharynx; enlarges in children causing nasal obstruction), lingual tonsil (base of tongue), and tubal tonsils (around Eustachian tube openings). All are MALT components covered by epithelium with crypts that trap antigens. They initiate mucosal immune responses without capsules.
visualDescription: Diagram or illustration showing the tonsillar ring in the pharynx: palatine tonsils, adenoid, and lingual tonsil locations

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the tonsils forming Waldeyer's ring at the entrance to the pharynx?

quizQuestion: Identify the two structural layers of the dermis shown in this histological section.
explanation: The dermis has two layers. The papillary dermis (superficial, ~20% of dermis thickness) consists of loose areolar connective tissue with thin collagen and elastic fibers, contains dermal papillae (increasing epidermal contact), capillary loops (nourishing avascular epidermis), and free nerve endings (Meissner's corpuscles for light touch). The reticular dermis (deep, ~80%) consists of dense irregular connective tissue with thick collagen bundles, contains hair follicles, sweat glands, sebaceous glands, Pacinian corpuscles (deep pressure/vibration), and Ruffini endings (stretch).
visualDescription: Histological section of skin showing the papillary dermis (loose connective tissue with dermal papillae) and reticular dermis (dense irregular connective tissue) below the epidermis

variants.quizQuestion: Which histological image shows the papillary and reticular layers of the dermis?

quizQuestion: Identify the different cell types found within the epidermis shown in this diagram.
explanation: The epidermis contains four main cell types: Keratinocytes (~90%) — synthesize keratin (structural protein), undergo terminal differentiation from stratum basale to stratum corneum, connected by desmosomes. Melanocytes (~8%) — neural crest-derived, located in stratum basale, produce melanin in melanosomes transferred to keratinocytes for UV protection. Langerhans cells (~2%) — bone marrow-derived dendritic cells in stratum spinosum, antigen-presenting cells of the skin. Merkel cells (<1%) — mechanoreceptors in stratum basale, slow-adapting type I, for fine touch discrimination.
visualDescription: Diagram of the epidermis highlighting the four cell types: keratinocytes, melanocytes, Langerhans cells, and Merkel cells with their characteristic appearances and locations

variants.quizQuestion: Which diagram shows the four epidermal cell types: keratinocytes, melanocytes, Langerhans cells, and Merkel cells?

quizQuestion: Identify the detailed cellular architecture of the outermost body covering shown here.
explanation: Detailed epidermal cellular anatomy shows keratinocytes in progressive stages of keratinization (cornification). In the stratum basale, mitotically active cells are attached to the basement membrane by hemidesmosomes. As cells move upward, they accumulate keratin intermediate filaments. In the stratum spinosum, cells are connected by desmosomes (giving the spiny appearance in fixed preparations). Lamellar granules (membrane-coating granules) are prominent in stratum granulosum — they secrete lipids (ceramides) that form the water-impermeable epidermal barrier.
visualDescription: High-magnification diagram of epidermal cells showing ultrastructural features: desmosomes, tonofilaments, lamellar granules, and basement membrane attachments

variants.quizQuestion: Which image shows the detailed cellular structure of the epidermis including desmosomes and lamellar granules?

quizQuestion: Identify the five layers of the outermost skin layer shown in this histological diagram.
explanation: The epidermis has five layers (from deep to superficial): Stratum basale (germinativum) — single layer of mitotically active columnar keratinocytes, contains melanocytes and Merkel cells. Stratum spinosum — several layers of polygonal keratinocytes with prominent desmosomes, contains Langerhans cells. Stratum granulosum — 3–5 layers with keratohyalin granules and lamellar granules. Stratum lucidum — present only in thick skin (palms, soles), translucent layer of dead cells. Stratum corneum — 15–30 layers of anucleate, dead cornified cells (corneocytes) — the barrier layer.
visualDescription: Histological diagram of thick skin epidermis labeling all five strata from stratum basale through stratum corneum

variants.quizQuestion: Which histological diagram shows all five epidermal strata from stratum basale to stratum corneum?

quizQuestion: Identify the specialized pigment-producing cells shown in this skin diagram.
explanation: Melanocytes are neural crest-derived cells residing in the stratum basale. They synthesize melanin (from tyrosine via tyrosinase) in organelles called melanosomes. Melanocytes donate melanosomes to surrounding keratinocytes via cytoplasmic dendrites ('melanocyte unit': 1 melanocyte serves ~36 keratinocytes). Melanin forms a supranuclear cap over keratinocyte nuclei, shielding DNA from UV radiation. Two types: eumelanin (brown-black) and pheomelanin (yellow-red). Melanocyte count is equal across races; skin color varies due to melanosome size, number, and distribution.
visualDescription: Diagram showing melanocytes in the stratum basale with long dendritic processes transferring melanosomes to surrounding keratinocytes

variants.quizQuestion: Which diagram shows melanocytes with dendritic processes transferring melanosomes to keratinocytes?

quizQuestion: Identify the organ system shown in this comprehensive cross-sectional illustration.
explanation: A comprehensive skin section shows three main layers: epidermis (stratified squamous keratinized epithelium — barrier and sensory function), dermis (connective tissue — structural support, vasculature, nerves, appendages), and hypodermis/subcutis (adipose connective tissue — insulation, energy storage, shock absorption). Skin appendages include hair follicles with arrector pili muscles, sebaceous glands (holocrine, secrete sebum), eccrine sweat glands (merocrine, thermoregulation), and apocrine sweat glands (axillary/genital, modified by bacteria into odor compounds). Nails are specialized keratinized structures.
visualDescription: Detailed cross-sectional illustration of skin showing the epidermis, dermis, and hypodermis with hair follicles, sebaceous glands, sweat glands, blood vessels, and sensory receptors

variants.quizQuestion: Which cross-sectional illustration shows the complete skin structure with epidermis, dermis, hypodermis, and appendages?
